2606.25176.title: Elo-Disentangled Player-Style Embeddings for Human Chess via Rating-Conditioned Residual Move Model
2606.25176.paper_summary: We study representation learning for individual human chess style: a per-player embedding learned from a player's move history such that inner products measure stylistic similarity, while being approximately disentangled from playing strength (Elo). Our key design is a residual formulation: a rating-conditioned base move model (Maia-3 policy logits plus Stockfish-derived features, scored over Maia-2-proposed candidates) captures what a typical player of a given strength would play, and a frozen copy of it anchors a learned move encoder and a per-player vector z, so that z explains only deviations from rating-typical play. The base model improves move prediction over the strong Maia-3 policy by 27-37% relative NLL across the rating spectrum, with the largest gains at the top (2800+); Stockfish's marginal value grows monotonically with Elo (negligible at 900-1200, +0.085 nats at 2800+). On a shared Elo-stratified benchmark of 22,620 held-out decisions, top-1 move-matching rises monotonically from Maia-2 to Maia-3 to the Stockfish-augmented base (0.51 -> 0.57 -> 0.68): the base is +33% relative top-1 over Maia-2 and +19% over Maia-3 (30% lower NLL), with the engine-feature lift largest at high Elo. The player embedding adds little to raw move-matching on top of this base -- its marginal top-1 gain falls within the 95% confidence interval -- and its value is instead representational: z generalizes to held-out decisions without overfitting, re-identifies players from disjoint games above chance, and a linear probe recovers rating from z with only R^2 = 0.06 (no better nonlinearly), evidence it captures style on an Elo-orthogonal axis. We argue that a strong rating-conditioned base plus a compact, Elo-disentangled embedding -- separating typical play from individual deviation -- is an economical, interpretable model of individual style, an alternative to per-player preference fine-tuning.
2606.25176.paper_summary_zh: 我們研究個別人類棋風的表示學習：從玩家的走棋歷史中學習的每位玩家嵌入，使得內積可以衡量風格相似性，同時與棋力（Elo）大致解耦。我們的關鍵設計是一種殘差公式：一個基於評分的基本走棋模型（Maia-3 策略邏輯加上 Stockfish 衍生特徵，針對 Maia-2 提議的候選者進行評分）捕捉了給定棋力的典型玩家會下的棋，而它的凍結副本則為學習的走棋編碼器和每位玩家的向量 z 提供了基礎，使得 z 僅解釋與評分典型走棋的偏差。該基本模型在整個評分範圍內，相對於強大的 Maia-3 策略，將走棋預測提升了 27-37% 的相對 NLL，最高增益出現在頂部（2800+）；Stockfish 的邊際價值隨著 Elo 單調增長（在 900-1200 時微不足道，在 2800+ 時為 +0.085 nats）。在一個包含 22,620 個保留決策的共享 Elo 分層基準上，前 1 名的走棋匹配率從 Maia-2 到 Maia-3 再到 Stockfish 增強的基礎單調上升（0.51 -> 0.57 -> 0.68）：該基礎相對於 Maia-2 提升了 +33% 的前 1 名，並且相對於 Maia-3 提升了 +19%（NLL 低 30%），而引擎特徵的提升在高 Elo 時最大。這個玩家嵌入在此基礎上對原始走棋匹配的貢獻不大——其邊際前 1 名增益落在 95% 置信區間內——而其價值則是表徵性的：z 在不過擬合的情況下對保留決策進行了泛化，從不相關的棋局中重新識別玩家，並且線性探測從 z 中恢復評分的 R^2 僅為 0.06（非線性情況下沒有更好），證據表明它捕捉了在與 Elo 正交的軸上的風格。我們認為，強大的基於評分的基礎加上緊湊的、與 Elo 解耦的嵌入——將典型走棋與個別偏差分開——是一種經濟、可解釋的個人風格模型，是每位玩家偏好微調的替代方案。
2606.25176.author: Jason Carlson et.al.
